body.question_1: How would you rate the technical competency of the student intern in their relevant field of study?
body.answer_1: The intern's technical competency revealed inadequate gaps between what the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering curriculum covers and the practical skills required in a professional workplace environment. While the intern possessed a reasonable theoretical grounding in core concepts, they struggled to translate this knowledge into practical application without significant guidance and hand-holding from senior team members. Proficiency in industry-standard tools and workflows was below the level we typically expect from graduates at a comparable stage, and several foundational technical skills needed to be reinforced on the job. We strongly recommend the School of Engineering review its curriculum to better align academic content with current industry tool and practice standards.

body.question_2: How well did the intern demonstrate professional communication and interpersonal skills in the workplace?
body.answer_2: The professional attitude demonstrated by this intern from the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering program was remarkable and genuinely impressed our team throughout the internship period. Punctuality, reliability, and a consistently positive and collaborative work ethic were evident from the very first week, requiring no correction or management intervention to maintain. The intern communicated proactively, raised concerns and questions through appropriate channels, and responded to feedback constructively and without defensiveness. Their professional maturity — particularly in managing competing deadlines, handling ambiguity, and engaging respectfully with colleagues at all levels — reflects a level of preparation that speaks well of the School of Engineering's commitment to producing work-ready graduates.

body.question_3: How effectively did the intern collaborate with team members and contribute to group projects?
body.answer_3: This intern's teamwork skills were moderate, with a genuine willingness to collaborate balanced by some developmental areas that became apparent as project complexity increased. The intern worked well in low-ambiguity team tasks with clear role definitions, but required additional support when navigating more complex team dynamics involving competing priorities, unclear ownership, or conflicting perspectives. Communication within the team improved noticeably over the internship duration, which is an encouraging sign of learning and adaptability. Further emphasis on collaborative project work, cross-functional team exercises, and conflict resolution skills in the Civil Engineering curriculum would produce more team-ready graduates for the professional environment.

body.question_4: How would you assess the intern's problem-solving abilities and approach to challenging or unfamiliar tasks?
body.answer_4: The intern's problem-solving abilities were among the most superb attributes they brought to our organization from the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering program. When confronted with unfamiliar challenges or technical obstacles, the intern approached them systematically — breaking problems into manageable components, forming and testing hypotheses, and escalating appropriately only when genuinely blocked by limitations beyond their current capability. Their intellectual curiosity, persistence under pressure, and willingness to explore unconventional solutions made them a genuinely valuable problem-solver within the team. This quality is difficult to teach in the workplace and speaks highly of the critical thinking culture embedded in the School of Engineering's academic programs.

body.question_5: How quickly and effectively did the intern adapt to your company's work culture, tools, and environment?
body.answer_5: The intern's adaptability to our workplace culture, processes, and evolving project requirements was impressive and demonstrated a level of professional resilience and flexibility that we consistently value in permanent hires. Changes to project scope, team structure, and delivery timelines were managed with composure, and the intern made adjustments to their work approach quickly and effectively without requiring extended support or reassurance from management. They were proactive in seeking to understand the context behind changes rather than simply executing instructions, which allowed them to contribute meaningfully even in fluid project environments. This quality reflects a well-rounded education at the School of Engineering that extends beyond technical training to genuine professional agility.

body.question_6: How proficient was the intern in English communication, both written and verbal, for professional purposes?
body.answer_6: English communication capability for this intern was decent, sufficient for routine internal tasks but requiring development to meet the standards expected in professional, client-facing, or international contexts. Reading comprehension of English-language materials was generally strong, enabling effective research and document processing independently. However, written composition — particularly formal reports and professional emails — required editing support, and verbal fluency in meetings was limited, particularly when discussions moved at pace or involved complex technical vocabulary. Strengthening English for Professional Purposes training in the Civil Engineering curriculum would meaningfully enhance graduates' communication readiness for the modern, increasingly international workplace.

body.question_7: How well did the intern's academic training align with the practical demands and standards of your industry?
body.answer_7: Our experience with this intern has highlighted insufficient gaps between what the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering curriculum delivers and what the professional industry actually requires from graduates in this field. Specifically, the intern lacked working proficiency in several tools and methodologies that are now industry standard, requiring us to invest significant onboarding time in areas that should be covered academically. Soft skills — particularly professional communication, project management, and stakeholder management — also appeared underdeveloped relative to what professional roles at this level require from day one. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with the School of Engineering's curriculum design team to provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement.

body.question_8: What specific technical or soft skill areas do you believe the university curriculum should strengthen for future graduates?
body.answer_8: Based on our experience with this intern from the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering program, we are commendablely impressed with the alignment between the academic curriculum and the practical demands of our industry. The foundational knowledge, theoretical frameworks, and applied skills the intern brought to the role mapped closely to what we require in graduate-level professional practitioners in this field. In particular, the project-based and problem-solving components of the curriculum appear to have translated well into workplace readiness. We would encourage the School of Engineering to continue developing its industry connections and to involve practitioners actively in curriculum review to maintain this strong alignment as the professional landscape continues to evolve.

body.question_9: How would you rate the intern's overall performance and value contribution to your organization during the placement?
body.answer_9: Overall, the intern's performance during this placement was problematic and fell below the standard we expected based on the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering program reputation. While the intern showed genuine enthusiasm and willingness to engage with assigned tasks, output quality was inconsistent, deadlines were occasionally missed without adequate advance communication, and independent contribution to team goals was limited relative to the support investment required from supervisors. We encourage the School of Engineering to strengthen the preparatory components of its internship program — including pre-placement coaching and more rigorous selection criteria — to better match student readiness with the expectations of professional host organizations.

body.question_10: Based on your experience with this intern, would you consider hiring from this university in the future, and why?
body.answer_10: Based on this intern's performance and the overall quality of graduates we have observed from the School of Engineering's Civil Engineering program, we would absolutely consider hiring from this institution in the future — both for internships and for permanent graduate positions. The intern's technical competency, professional attitude, collaborative skills, and growth mindset align closely with the profile we seek when making graduate hiring decisions. We are actively discussing a return internship offer for this individual and have already flagged the School of Engineering as a preferred graduate source institution for our talent acquisition team. We would encourage the School of Engineering to continue deepening its industry partnerships and look forward to an ongoing and mutually beneficial relationship with the institution in the years ahead.
